page: Amazon Web Services a1.xlarge is an ARM-based instance powered by the first-generation AWS Graviton processor. It features 4 dedicated physical cores using the ARMv8 Cortex-A72 architecture, running at a clock speed of 2.3 GHz with one thread per core. The instance is equipped with 8.0 GB of system memory, providing 2.0 GB per core, and operates on the AWS Nitro hypervisor. It does not include local storage or GPU hardware, and it provides a baseline network bandwidth of 1 Gbps.

Benchmark data indicates that the a1.xlarge delivers average performance in stress-ng single-core and multi-core CPU tests. However, it falls into the bottom decile for memory bandwidth, with read-only large block speeds at 12.56 GB/sec and write speeds at 6.77 GB/sec. Cryptographic and compression tasks, such as single-threaded gzip and bzip3 decompression, also register in the lower performance tier. Geekbench and Passmark CPU scores reflect these limitations, particularly in workloads requiring advanced vector or matrix extensions, which are not supported by this hardware generation.

From a resource-density perspective, the a1.xlarge offers a low-cost entry point into ARM64 development and testing. The dedicated core allocation ensures consistent CPU access without the credit-depletion risks of burstable instances. However, the tradeoff is a significantly lower performance-per-resource ratio compared to newer processor generations, especially for memory-bound or instruction-heavy applications.

This instance is suited for lightweight, scale-out workloads that do not require high memory bandwidth or advanced instruction sets. Appropriate use cases include basic web serving, microservices, development and testing environments for ARM64 architectures, and simple queue processing.
description: The Amazon Web Services a1.xlarge is an ARM64-based instance utilizing the first-generation AWS Graviton processor. It features 4 dedicated physical cores built on the ARMv8 Cortex-A72 architecture running at 2.3 GHz, paired with 8.0 GB of system memory and a 1 Gbps baseline network interface. Operating on the Nitro hypervisor, this instance does not include local storage or GPU accelerators. Benchmark results show average CPU performance in stress-ng tests, but poor memory bandwidth and weak execution in cryptographic, compression, and database operations. The a1.xlarge serves as a cost-effective entry point for ARM64 development, microservices, and basic web serving, though it presents performance tradeoffs for memory-intensive or instruction-heavy workloads.
og_description: Amazon Web Services a1.xlarge is an ARM64 instance featuring 4 dedicated Graviton cores, 8.0 GB of memory, and a 1 Gbps network baseline, designed for cost-effective ARM64 development and lightweight workloads.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services a1.xlarge is an ARM64 instance with 4 dedicated Graviton cores and 8.0 GB of RAM, suited for lightweight web serving and testing.
tagline: A dedicated ARM64 instance featuring four physical cores and eight gigabytes of memory for cost-efficient development and lightweight scale-out workloads.
bullet_points: Four dedicated ARMv8 Cortex-A72 physical cores running at 2.3 GHz
8.0 GB of system memory providing a 1:2 vCPU-to-RAM ratio
AWS Nitro hypervisor integration with 1 Gbps baseline network bandwidth
No local storage or GPU accelerators included
Suited for ARM64 software development, testing, and basic web serving
